Project Poster

IS428 AY2025/26 Term 2 — Submitted 8 April 2026

Poster

Our A1 poster was submitted on 8 April 2026 and presented at the class poster session on 13 April 2026. It summarises the four key findings and the visual evidence supporting each one.

[image]

Full A1 poster — 300 dpi JPEG

Poster Sections

Problem Statement

COOTEFOO oversees fishing and tourism policy in Oceanus. Two lobby groups — FILAH and TROUT — submitted conflicting datasets, each claiming the committee is biased against their industry. A journalist obtained the complete record. This project uses visual analytics to determine which narrative, if any, is supported by the data.

Methodology

  • Knowledge graph transformation using Python (mc2_prepare.py)
  • Bipartite network analysis in Gephi (3 datasets)
  • Tableau dashboards with interactive dataset filters
  • Bias score formula: (tourism − fishing) / total participations

Key Findings

Finding 1: Both lobby datasets are curated products. FILAH captured 45% of all records; TROUT captured 31%. Both systematically over-represent their preferred industry.

Finding 2: The full committee is balanced. No member’s bias score is statistically extreme when using the journalist’s complete record.

Finding 3: Sentiment and travel show no systematic tilt. Meeting sentiment oscillates around neutral across 16 sessions.

Finding 4: Tante Titan — 54 records, most active member, tourism-leaning — was excluded from both lobby datasets. Her exclusion creates the apparent bias discrepancy.

Poster Session Feedback

The poster was presented at the IS428 class session on 13 April 2026. Four groups provided written evaluations.

What worked well:

  • The core message — bias comes from selective omission, not fabricated data — was clearly communicated and consistently understood across all evaluators
  • Key insights panels were highlighted as an effective way to draw quick conclusions before engaging with the group
  • The lollipop/bias bar chart was noted for giving a clear picture of each member’s sentiment range at a glance
  • Good variety of chart types and effective use of colour and legends

Areas for improvement:

  • A brief context introduction was missing — several evaluators needed to ask for background before they could interpret the poster
  • The sentiment heatmap was difficult to read due to many null values; a cleaner chart or alternative visual was suggested
  • Some charts were too small to read comfortably at poster size
  • Colour palette was perceived as inconsistent across the project
  • Axis titles on the sentiment chart were not renamed from defaults
  • Future work section could be expanded with more depth
